answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: The sector group chose three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios. These were: Orderly Category: Net Zero 2025, Disorderly Category: Delayed Transition and Hot House World Category: Current Policies. [...] IPCC scenarios: SSP1-1.9/RCP1.9 [Net Zero 2050], SSP1-2.6/RCP2.6 [Delayed Transition], SSP3-7.0/RCP7.0 [Current Policies]. CCC scenarios: Tailwinds [Net Zero 2050], Headwinds [Delayed Transition], Current Policy Reference [Current Policies]. [...] A baseline risk assessment representing 1.1°C of global warming [...] Two further scenarios representing 1.5°C and 3.0°C of global warming.
answers.notes: The three NGFS-based sector scenarios are the primary framework; IPCC SSP/RCP and NZ CCC scenarios are mapped to them. The 1.1°C baseline, 1.5°C and 3.0°C are used for the entity-level risk assessment.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Net Zero 2050 (Orderly): 1.6°C in 2050, reducing to 1.4°C by 2100. Delayed Transition (Disorderly): 1.7°C in 2050, reducing to 1.6°C by 2100. Current Policies (Hot House World): 2°C by 2050 and more than 3°C by 2100. Baseline: 1.1°C. Entity-level risk assessment scenarios: 1.5°C and 3.0°C.
answers.evidence: An ambitious and coordinated transition to a low-emissions, climate-resilient future. Stringent climate policies, innovation, ambitious investment, and medium-to-high deployment of carbon removal solutions limit global warming to 1.6°C in 2050 and reducing to 1.4°C by 2100. Ambitious action is delayed to 2030, followed by sudden and uncoordinated economic transformation. Extensive, stringent and punitive but late government intervention, in combination with some deployment of carbon removal solutions, limits global warming to 1.7°C in 2050 and reducing to 1.6°C by 2100. Current emissions reduction policies are implemented. Current socio-economic trends continue, resulting in 2°C global warming by 2050 and more than 3°C by 2100.
answers.notes: Temperature overshoot is implied in the Orderly scenario (peaks at 1.6°C in 2050 then reduces to 1.4°C by 2100). No explicit use of the word "overshoot" in the document.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are explicitly named: (1) Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) — basis for the three sector-level scenarios; (2) Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) — SSP/RCP scenarios mapped to each; (3) New Zealand Climate Change Commission (CCC) — NZ-specific scenarios mapped to each; (4) KPMG New Zealand — co-developed the retail sector scenarios ("The Futures of Retail").
answers.evidence: The sector group chose three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios. [...] Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) scenarios: Shared socio-economic Pathway (SSP)-Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) SSP1-1.9/RCP1.9 [...] New Zealand Climate Change Commission (CCC) scenarios: Tailwinds [...] In 2023, we collaborated with other New Zealand retailers that are climate reporting entities and KPMG New Zealand to co-design a set of integrated climate change scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Shared socio-economic Pathway (SSP)-Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) SSP1-1.9/RCP1.9 [Net Zero 2050], SSP1-2.6/RCP2.6 [Delayed Transition], SSP3-7.0/RCP7.0 [Current Policies]
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP and RCP identifiers together (e.g. SSP1-1.9/RCP1.9, SSP1-2.6/RCP2.6, SSP3-7.0/RCP7.0). These pairings combine SSP socioeconomic pathways with RCP radiative forcing levels. Whether these are internally consistent IPCC AR6 pairings or a mix across scenario families requires expert judgment; the document presents them as paired identifiers without further methodological explanation.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: In 2023, we collaborated with other New Zealand retailers that are climate reporting entities and KPMG New Zealand to co-design a set of integrated climate change scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector. These scenarios are detailed in a published report entitled "The Futures of Retail" published on the KPMG website. The work included the development of three climate-related scenario narratives over three time-horizons for each retailer to consider when developing their own climate scenarios. The sector group chose three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios. [...] A retail sector narrative was formed for each scenario identifying the critical interactions and key outcomes and indicators.
answers.notes: The NGFS scenarios were adapted into a New Zealand retail sector context, with sector-specific narratives developed. The document also describes a Briscoe Group-specific risk assessment layered on top of these sector scenarios, further customising them to entity-level business planning timeframes.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: In 2023, we collaborated with other New Zealand retailers that are climate reporting entities and KPMG New Zealand to co-design a set of integrated climate change scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector.
answers.notes: The document explicitly uses the term "co-design" and describes collaboration between multiple NZ retailers and KPMG New Zealand to develop the sector scenarios. However, the document also notes: "Other than our experts mentioned above, we did not engage any other external partners or stakeholders in the process" for the FY25 review.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes. A baseline risk assessment representing 1.1°C of global warming is described, used to identify current physical and transition impacts already incurred.
answers.evidence: A baseline risk assessment representing 1.1°C of global warming helping us to identify the current physical and transition impacts we have incurred.
answers.notes: The 1.1°C baseline represents current warming conditions and is used as the reference point against which the 1.5°C and 3.0°C scenarios are compared.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: The first iteration of our Scenario analysis, climate-risk assessment, and transition planning has been performed as a stand-alone process, no modelling was undertaken, and it was not integrated into our usual strategy processes.
answers.notes: The document explicitly states that no modelling was undertaken. No climate models, IAMs, or macroeconomic models are named.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Increased storminess/ extreme winds River and pluvial flooding [...] Increase in storminess (frequency, intensity) including tropical cyclones. Changes in extreme wind speed. Increase in convective weather events (tornadoes, lightning). Changes in extremes: high intensity and persistence of rainfall. Increase in hail severity or frequency. There will be an increasing incidence of storm events with increasing severity impacting supply chains and operations. Potential store closures. Delays in supply chain. Staff and customers unable to get to our stores. Loss of sales and decrease in profit. Cost of repairs/maintenance to buildings. Increased lease costs. Increased supply chain costs. Increased insurance premiums. [...] Sea level rise of 0.32m could impact specific locations and increase losses due to flooding. Temporary store closures.
answers.notes: The document identifies acute physical risks (storms, flooding, cyclones) and describes their potential financial impacts (loss of sales, repair costs, insurance premiums). However, no quantified monetary damage/loss estimates are provided — impacts are described qualitatively.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: financial impact of supply chain disruptions
answers.evidence: Increase in storminess (frequency, intensity) including tropical cyclones. Changes in extreme wind speed. [...] Changes in extremes: high intensity and persistence of rainfall. [...] Sea level rise of 0.32m could impact specific locations [...] Consumer sentiment [...] Macro-economic conditions [...] Policy reaction to climate change [...] Regional policy variation [...] Speed of technology change [...] Financial impact of supply chain disruptions
answers.notes: These variables appear in the scenario comparison table and in the risk assessment sections. No explicit carbon price variable is mentioned; the document notes "We do not currently use an internal emissions price."

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: In 2023, we collaborated with other New Zealand retailers that are climate reporting entities and KPMG New Zealand to co-design a set of integrated climate change scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector. [...] The sector group chose three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios. [...] A retail sector narrative was formed for each scenario identifying the critical interactions and key outcomes and indicators. These scenarios considered three different time horizons: short (2023-2030), medium (2031-2040) and long (2041-2050) and explored the political, environmental, societal, technological, legal and economic impacts across each potential pathway.
answers.notes: The scenarios are based on global NGFS/IPCC scenarios but have been adapted to a New Zealand retail sector context, making them both global in origin and regional/local in application. The document also references New Zealand-specific CCC scenarios. No explicit mention of formal "downscaling" of climate models.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Three time horizons are used: Short-term (1–3 years), Medium-term (>3 to 10 years), Long-term (>10 years). The sector scenarios also used: short (2023–2030), medium (2031–2040), long (2041–2050). A rationale is provided: timeframes were aligned to entity-level business planning and investment timeframes.
answers.evidence: The sector-based time horizons which look out to 2050 were used in the workshops to provide guidance, however, an important objective of the workshops was to align risks and opportunities to entity level business planning and investment timeframes of: Short-term: 1 to 3 years; Medium-term: >3 to 10 years; Long term: > 10years. [...] These scenarios considered three different time horizons: short (2023-2030), medium (2031-2040) and long (2041-2050).
answers.notes: The rationale given is alignment with entity-level business planning and investment timeframes. No further explanation is provided for why these specific year ranges were chosen.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.answer: Yes — partial justification is provided. The use of a combination of scenarios is justified as adding resilience to the risk assessment process. The choice of NGFS scenarios as the basis is stated but not deeply explained beyond their use as a recognised framework.
answers.evidence: Using a combination of scenarios was intended to add resilience to the risk assessment process and the resultant strategy as we prepare for inevitable uncertainty in the short to medium-term.
answers.notes: The justification is limited — the document explains why a combination of scenarios was used (resilience to uncertainty) but does not provide a detailed rationale for why the specific NGFS scenarios, IPCC SSP/RCP pairings, or CCC scenarios were selected over alternatives.
